papergraph.title: Research on Power Stability Control Technology of Smart Grid under Security Reinforcement Learning
papergraph.citation: Yingda Chen et al. (2025) Research on Power Stability Control Technology of Smart Grid under Security Reinforcement Learning doi:10.1109/cepsi66359.2025.11403832
papergraph.venue: 2025 25th Conference of the Electric Power Supply Industry (CEPSI)
papergraph.abstract: This study focuses on power stability control technology in smart grids under safe reinforcement learning, aiming to enhance the grid’s adaptability to complex operating environments and improve the precision and stability of power regulation. With the increasing uncertainty of renewable energy integration and load variations, traditional stability control technologies face limitations in response speed and adaptability. Based on an analysis of power fluctuation characteristics and the shortcomings of conventional stability control methods, this paper introduces a safe reinforcement learning algorithm. This algorithm continuously learns and optimizes through interactions between the agent and the grid environment to achieve precise power control. Unlike traditional methods that rely on mathematical modeling or rule-based control, reinforcement learning autonomously learns optimal strategies, enhancing the flexibility and adaptability of the stability control system. To ensure grid security, this study designs corresponding safety mechanisms, including the construction of safety constraints, risk assessment models, and protective barriers to prevent erroneous strategies during learning from compromising grid stability. The proposed method effectively suppresses power fluctuations under various operating conditions. Compared with traditional PID control, it reduces frequency deviation by approximately 30% and shortens frequency recovery time by 25%. Compared with MPC control, it decreases the number of voltage limit violations by 67% and improves the load recovery rate by over 4%. These results demonstrate that the proposed safe reinforcement learning approach outperforms traditional methods in terms of dynamic response speed, steady-state performance, and safety robustness, significantly enhancing grid stability under complex disturbances. The findings provide an innovative solution for smart grid stability control and offer technical support for intelligent applications such as renewable energy integration and grid dispatch optimization.

improves load recovery rate by over 4%

papergraph.title: Stability Constrained Reinforcement Learning for Real-Time Voltage Control
papergraph.citation: Yuanyuan Shi et al. (2021) Stability Constrained Reinforcement Learning for Real-Time Voltage Control doi:10.23919/ACC53348.2022.9867476
papergraph.abstract: Deep reinforcement learning (RL) has been recognized as a promising tool to address the challenges in real-time control of power systems. However, its deployment in real-world power systems has been hindered by a lack of formal stability and safety guarantees. In this paper, we propose a stability constrained reinforcement learning method for real-time voltage control in distribution grids and we prove that the proposed approach provides a formal voltage stability guarantee. The key idea underlying our approach is an explicitly constructed Lyapunov function that certifies stability. We demonstrate the effectiveness of the approach in case studies, where the proposed method can reduce the transient control cost by more than 30% and shorten the response time by a third compared to a widely used linear policy, while always achieving voltage stability. In contrast, standard RL methods often fail to achieve voltage stability.

papergraph.title: Deep Reinforcement Learning For Autonomous Control Of Low Voltage Grids With Focus On Grid Stability In Future Power Grids
papergraph.citation: Lars Quakernack et al. (2022) Deep Reinforcement Learning For Autonomous Control Of Low Voltage Grids With Focus On Grid Stability In Future Power Grids doi:10.1109/ISGT-Europe54678.2022.9960416
papergraph.venue: IEEE PES Innovative Smart Grid Technologies Conference Europe
papergraph.abstract: The effort to achieve a low-emission electrical grid in Germany poses challenges for distribution system operators. These include the expansion of volatile and decentralised re-newable energy resources (DER), such as photovoltaics, and the steady expansion of electromobility with high charging capacities in the low voltage (LV) grid. To enhance the grid stability and to avoid costly grid expansion, a autonomous control systems must be implemented. For this purpose a double deep Q-learning (DDQN) approach with multiple agents are being investigated. The reinforcement learning (RL) agents are being trained and validated on a LV grid with grid instabilities such as voltage drops, transformer overload and reverse power flow to the medium voltage level. The grid consists of 13 nodes, 1 string with 11 households, 11 PV systems with battery storage systems (BSS) and 11 electrical vehicle (EV). As results the voltage level drops are decrease from 0.89 p.u. to 0.95 p.u. compared between controlled and uncontrolled cases. Also, a reverse power flow can be fully prevented. The transformer utilisation can be decreased by a maximum of 1 p.u.. Moreover, the batteries are more often utilised for global grid stability purposes then optimise local self consumption.

papergraph.title: Provable Stability of Reinforcement Learning-Based Frequency Control in Low-Inertia Power Systems
papergraph.citation: Hamad Alduaij et al. (2025) Provable Stability of Reinforcement Learning-Based Frequency Control in Low-Inertia Power Systems doi:10.1109/SmartGridComm65349.2025.11204642
papergraph.venue: IEEE International Conference on Smart Grid Communications
papergraph.abstract: Modern converter-dominated power grids require fast and adaptive frequency control while guaranteeing system stability and safety. However, existing reinforcement learning (RL) approaches for inverter-based control often sacrifice theoretical guarantees for adaptivity, making them unsuitable for safety-critical grid operations. In this paper, we propose a physics-guided control decomposition that decouples the stability and adaptation mechanisms within a provably stable RL framework. The controller comprises two components: (i) a strongly monotonic nominal control which is used as a baseline that guarantees exponential stability via Lyapunov energy decay, and (ii) a sign-controlled residual that enables robust adaptation to unknown and time-varying inertia and damping. We derive explicit design margins that certify local exponential stability under actuator saturation and parameter variations. The resulting method maintains linear computational complexity and is suitable for real-time deployment on grid-forming inverters. Simulation results on the IEEE 39-bus system show that our method achieves faster settling time, reduced frequency overshoot, and improved robustness to parameter drift compared to safe RL baselines such as Lyapunov RL techniques. As a result, this work provides a scalable and theoretically grounded framework for learning-based frequency control in low-inertia power systems.

papergraph.title: Research on optimization of power grid load forecasting models based on deep learning
papergraph.citation: Junhua Hu et al. (2026) Research on optimization of power grid load forecasting models based on deep learning. doi:10.1038/s41598-026-47517-3
papergraph.abstract: Precise forecasting of power grid load is essential for maintaining the stability and efficiency of contemporary energy systems. Traditional statistical and machine learning methods often struggle to capture the nonlinear temporal dependencies and dynamic variations in power loads, limiting their predictive accuracy. To overcome these difficulties, this study proposes an Adaptive Deep Load Predictor (ADLP) that integrates hybrid temporal encoding, a dynamic attention mechanism, and an online adaptive updating strategy. By integrating bidirectional long short-term memory networks for temporal sequence analysis with convolutional neural networks for extracting spatial features, ADLP effectively captures both short-term fluctuations and long-term dependencies in load patterns. The dynamic attention mechanism further enhances predictive performance by assigning adaptive importance to historical data, while the online adaptive updating module ensures real-time adaptability to evolving load conditions. This study introduces a Dynamic Grid-Aware Load Optimization (DGLO) strategy to enhance power grid operations based on real-time predictive insights. It integrates predictive adaptive control, stochastic robust optimization, and energy storage integration to dynamically adjust grid operations. The predictive adaptive control mechanism refines real-time generation adjustments using deep learning-based forecasting and proportional-integral-derivative control. The stochastic robust optimization component compensates for uncertainties in load variations, improving system resilience. The energy storage integration module optimizes charge-discharge cycles to stabilize energy utilization. Extensive experiments on real-world datasets demonstrate the superiority of the proposed framework over traditional and existing deep learning methods. The results confirm its enhanced forecasting precision, adaptability, and robustness in smart grid applications. Future work will focus on improving computational efficiency, integrating reinforcement learning, and expanding generalization across different grid environments.

Abstract: This study focuses on power stability control technology in smart grids under safe reinforcement learning, aiming to enhance the grid’s adaptability to complex operating environments and improve the precision and stability of power regulation. With the increasing uncertainty of renewable e...[truncated]
Full text excerpt:

This study focuses on power stability control technology in smart grids under safe reinforcement learning, aiming to enhance the grid’s adaptability to complex operating environments and improve the precision and stability of power regulation.
With the increasing uncertainty of renewable energy integration and load variations, traditional stability control technologies face limitations in response speed and adaptability.
Based on an analysis of power fluctuation characteristics and the shortcomings of conventional stability control methods, this paper introduces a safe reinforcement learning algorithm.
This algorithm continuously learns and optimizes through interactions between the agent and the grid environment to achieve precise power control.

Unlike traditional methods that rely on mathematical modeling or rule-based control, reinforcement learning autonomously learns optimal strategies, enhancing the flexibility and adaptability of the stability control system.
The proposed method effectively suppresses power fluctuations under various operating conditions.
Compared with MPC control, it decreases the number of voltage limit violations by 67% and improves the load recovery rate by over 4%.

These results demonstrate that the proposed safe reinforcement learning approach outperforms traditional methods in terms of dynamic response speed, steady-state performance, and safety robustness, significantly enhancing grid stability under complex disturbances.

evidence_type_annotations.claim: reinforcement learning
A reinforcement learning method with formal voltage stability guarantee via Lyapunov-based certification.

Abstract: Deep reinforcement learning (RL) has been recognized as a promising tool to address the challenges in real-time control of power systems. However, its deployment in real-world power systems has been hindered by a lack of formal stability and safety guarantees. In this paper, we propose a s...[truncated]
Full text excerpt:

In this paper, we propose a stability constrained reinforcement learning method for real-time voltage control in distribution grids and we prove that the proposed approach provides a formal voltage stability guarantee.
The key idea underlying our approach is an explicitly constructed Lyapunov function that certifies stability.

We demonstrate the effectiveness of the approach in case studies, where the proposed method can reduce the transient control cost by more than 30% and shorten the response time by a third compared to a widely used linear policy, while always achieving voltage stability.
In contrast, standard RL methods often fail to achieve voltage stability.

a scalable and theoretically grounded framework for learning-based frequency control that guarantees local exponential stability under actuator saturation and parameter variations

The controller comprises two components: (i) a strongly monotonic nominal control which is used as a baseline that guarantees exponential stability via Lyapunov energy decay, and (ii) a sign-controlled residual that enables robust adaptation to unknown and time-varying inertia and damping.
Full text excerpt:
However, existing reinforcement learning (RL) approaches for inverter-based control often sacrifice theoretical guarantees for adaptivity, making them unsuitable for safety-critical grid operations.
In this paper, we propose a physics-guided control decomposition that decouples the stability and adaptation mechanisms within a provably stable RL framework.
We derive explicit design margins that certify local exponential stability under actuator saturation and parameter variations.
The resulting method maintains linear computational complexity and is suitable for real-time deployment on grid-forming inverters.

Simulation results on the IEEE 39-bus system show that our method achieves faster settling time, reduced frequency overshoot, and improved robustness to parameter drift compared to safe RL baselines such as Lyapunov RL techniques.
As a result, this work provides a scalable and theoretically grounded framework for learning-based frequency control in low-inertia power systems.

With the increasing uncertainty of renewable e...[truncated]
Full text excerpt:

This study focuses on power stability control technology in smart grids under safe reinforcement learning, aiming to enhance the grid’s adaptability to complex operating environments and improve the precision and stability of power regulation.
With the increasing uncertainty of renewable energy integration and load variations, traditional stability control technologies face limitations in response speed and adaptability.
Based on an analysis of power fluctuation characteristics and the shortcomings of conventional stability control methods, this paper introduces a safe reinforcement learning algorithm.
This algorithm continuously learns and optimizes through interactions between the agent and the grid environment to achieve precise power control.

Unlike traditional methods that rely on mathematical modeling or rule-based control, reinforcement learning autonomously learns optimal strategies, enhancing the flexibility and adaptability of the stability control system.
The proposed method effectively suppresses power fluctuations under various operating conditions.
Compared with MPC control, it decreases the number of voltage limit violations by 67% and improves the load recovery rate by over 4%.

These results demonstrate that the proposed safe reinforcement learning approach outperforms traditional methods in terms of dynamic response speed, steady-state performance, and safety robustness, significantly enhancing grid stability under complex disturbances.

evidence_type_annotations.claim: reinforcement learning
A reinforcement learning method with formal voltage stability guarantee via Lyapunov-based certification.

Abstract: Deep reinforcement learning (RL) has been recognized as a promising tool to address the challenges in real-time control of power systems. However, its deployment in real-world power systems has been hindered by a lack of formal stability and safety guarantees. In this paper, we propose a s...[truncated]
Full text excerpt:

In this paper, we propose a stability constrained reinforcement learning method for real-time voltage control in distribution grids and we prove that the proposed approach provides a formal voltage stability guarantee.
The key idea underlying our approach is an explicitly constructed Lyapunov function that certifies stability.

We demonstrate the effectiveness of the approach in case studies, where the proposed method can reduce the transient control cost by more than 30% and shorten the response time by a third compared to a widely used linear policy, while always achieving voltage stability.
In contrast, standard RL methods often fail to achieve voltage stability.

method_scenario_benchmark_triples.benchmark: settling time | frequency overshoot | robustness to parameter drift
references: Hamad Alduaij et al. (2025) Provable Stability of Reinforcement Learning-Based Frequency Control in Low-Inertia Power Systems doi:10.1109/SmartGridComm65349.2025.11204642

evidence_type_annotations.claim: reinforcement learning
a scalable and theoretically grounded framework for learning-based frequency control that guarantees local exponential stability under actuator saturation and parameter variations

The controller comprises two components: (i) a strongly monotonic nominal control which is used as a baseline that guarantees exponential stability via Lyapunov energy decay, and (ii) a sign-controlled residual that enables robust adaptation to unknown and time-varying inertia and damping.
Full text excerpt:
However, existing reinforcement learning (RL) approaches for inverter-based control often sacrifice theoretical guarantees for adaptivity, making them unsuitable for safety-critical grid operations.
In this paper, we propose a physics-guided control decomposition that decouples the stability and adaptation mechanisms within a provably stable RL framework.
We derive explicit design margins that certify local exponential stability under actuator saturation and parameter variations.
The resulting method maintains linear computational complexity and is suitable for real-time deployment on grid-forming inverters.

Simulation results on the IEEE 39-bus system show that our method achieves faster settling time, reduced frequency overshoot, and improved robustness to parameter drift compared to safe RL baselines such as Lyapunov RL techniques.
As a result, this work provides a scalable and theoretically grounded framework for learning-based frequency control in low-inertia power systems.

Full text excerpt:
Traditional statistical and machine learning methods often struggle to capture the nonlinear temporal dependencies and dynamic variations in power loads, limiting their predictive accuracy.
Extensive experiments on real-world datasets demonstrate the superiority of the proposed framework over traditional and existing deep learning methods.
The results confirm its enhanced forecasting precision, adaptability, and robustness in smart grid applications.
